era_context: The early 16th century was a period of European Renaissance, religious ferment, and the beginning of global maritime expansion. The Protestant Reformation was in its infancy; the Spanish Empire was consolidating power under Charles V; and European explorers were beginning to venture into the Atlantic and beyond. In Asia, the Ming Dynasty ruled China; the Ottoman Empire was consolidating power; and the Aztec Empire in Mesoamerica was at its height, unaware of the Spanish threat gathering on its coasts.

events.title: Hernán Cortés Lands in Mexico; Spanish Conquest of Aztec Empire Begins
events.description: In February 1519, Spanish conquistador Hernán Cortés, commanding an expedition of approximately 600 men, landed on the coast of Mexico (in the region of present-day Veracruz). Cortés had been commissioned by the Spanish governor of Cuba to explore and trade, but he quickly moved inland toward the Aztec capital of Tenochtitlan. Along the way, he formed alliances with indigenous groups (particularly the Tlaxcalans) who were resentful of Aztec tribute demands. Cortés's expedition, though small in numbers, was equipped with horses, firearms, and steel weapons—technologies unknown to the Aztecs. By November 1519, Cortés had reached Tenochtitlan and was received by the Aztec emperor Montezuma II. This encounter initiated the Spanish conquest of the Aztec Empire, which would be completed by 1521.
key_figures: Hernán Cortés (Spanish conquistador)
Montezuma II (Aztec emperor)
Malinche (indigenous woman, translator and advisor to Cortés)
Tlaxcaltecatl (Tlaxcalan leader, ally of Cortés)

sources.name: Bernal Díaz del Castillo, The True History of the Conquest of New Spain (Historia verdadera de la conquista de la Nueva España)
Cortés, Cartas de relación (Letters of Relation to Charles V)
Florentine Codex (Sahagún's compilation of Nahua accounts)

events.certainty_note: Cortés's landing in February 1519 and his arrival in Tenochtitlan in November 1519 are well-documented in Spanish sources and confirmed by indigenous accounts. The dates are universally accepted.

events.title: Charles V Elected Holy Roman Emperor; Becomes Ruler of Spain and the Empire
events.description: On 28 June 1519, Charles V (also known as Charles I of Spain) was elected Holy Roman Emperor by the German princes. Charles was the grandson of both Isabella and Ferdinand of Spain and of Maximilian I, Holy Roman Emperor. His election as Holy Roman Emperor, combined with his inheritance of the Spanish throne (which he had received in 1516), made him the most powerful ruler in Europe. Charles's empire stretched from Spain and the Spanish colonies in the Americas to the Netherlands, Austria, and parts of Italy. His election marked the beginning of the Habsburg dominance of European politics that would last for centuries. The election was contested and expensive, requiring significant bribes to the German princes, but Charles's victory was decisive.
key_figures: Charles V (Holy Roman Emperor and King of Spain)
Maximilian I (Charles's grandfather, previous Holy Roman Emperor)
Isabella and Ferdinand (Charles's maternal grandparents, Spanish monarchs)
Francis I (King of France, rival for the imperial throne)

sources.name: Official records of the Imperial Diet and election

events.certainty_note: Charles V's election as Holy Roman Emperor on 28 June 1519 is well-documented in official imperial records and contemporary diplomatic correspondence. The date and the fact of his election are universally accepted.

events.title: Ferdinand Magellan's Expedition Departs Spain; Begins First Circumnavigation of the Globe
events.description: On 20 September 1519, Ferdinand Magellan, a Portuguese navigator in the service of Spain, departed from Seville with a fleet of five ships and approximately 270 men. Magellan's expedition was commissioned by Spanish King Charles V to find a westward route to the Spice Islands (the Moluccas) in Southeast Asia. The expedition sailed across the Atlantic, rounded Cape Horn at the southern tip of South America (discovering the Strait of Magellan in the process), and entered the Pacific Ocean. The voyage was extraordinarily difficult, with the crew suffering from scurvy, starvation, and disease. Magellan himself was killed in the Philippines in 1521, but the expedition continued under Juan Sebastián Elcano and returned to Spain in 1522, completing the first circumnavigation of the globe.
key_figures: Ferdinand Magellan (Portuguese explorer in Spanish service)
Juan Sebastián Elcano (Spanish navigator, took command after Magellan's death)
Charles V (Spanish king who sponsored the expedition)
Antonio Pigafetta (Italian chronicler of the voyage)

sources.name: Antonio Pigafetta, First Voyage Around the World (Primo viaggio intorno al mondo)

events.certainty_note: Magellan's departure from Seville on 20 September 1519 is well-documented in Spanish records and Pigafetta's account. The date is universally accepted.

events.title: Leonardo da Vinci Dies in France; End of Renaissance Master's Life
events.description: On 2 May 1519, Leonardo da Vinci, the quintessential Renaissance master of art, science, and engineering, died at the Château du Cloux in Amboise, France, at the age of 67. Leonardo had spent his final years in France under the patronage of King Francis I, who greatly admired him. Leonardo's death marked the end of an era of Renaissance genius. His notebooks, filled with anatomical drawings, engineering designs, and artistic studies, would profoundly influence subsequent generations of artists and scientists, though many of his works remained unpublished during his lifetime. Leonardo's legacy as an artist (Mona Lisa, The Last Supper) and as a proto-scientist and engineer was immense.
key_figures: Leonardo da Vinci (Renaissance master)
Francis I (King of France, Leonardo's patron)
Michelangelo (contemporary Renaissance master)
events.certainty_note: Leonardo da Vinci's death on 2 May 1519 is well-documented in contemporary accounts and French court records. The date is universally accepted.

events.title: Zheng He's Treasure Fleet Era Definitively Ended; Ming Dynasty Ceases Oceanic Voyages
events.description: By 1519, it was clear that the Ming Dynasty had permanently abandoned the great oceanic voyages of Zheng He (which had ended in 1433). The Confucian scholar-officials who dominated the Ming court had successfully argued against further maritime expeditions, viewing them as wasteful and contrary to Confucian principles of inward focus and land-based defense. The treasure ships were allowed to decay, and the shipyards that had built them were repurposed. This decision represented a fundamental reorientation of Chinese policy away from maritime exploration and toward continental concerns, particularly defense against Mongol incursions. The consequences of this choice would be profound: China withdrew from global maritime trade and exploration, ceding dominance of the seas to European powers.
key_figures: Zheng He (admiral, voyages ended 1433)
Confucian scholar-officials (advocates of retrenchment)
Ming emperors (who accepted the scholar-official consensus)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)
Historiographic consensus on Ming naval policy

events.certainty_note: The broader policy of maritime retrenchment is well-documented, though the specific year 1519 is somewhat arbitrary. The decision was a gradual process spanning decades.

events.title: Ulrich Zwingli Begins Protestant Reformation in Zurich
events.description: In 1519, Ulrich Zwingli, a Swiss priest and humanist, began preaching sermons in Zurich that challenged Catholic doctrine and advocated for religious reform. Zwingli's reformation was independent of Martin Luther's movement in Germany, though both were influenced by Renaissance humanism and the desire to return to biblical Christianity. Zwingli's reforms were more radical than Luther's in some respects, particularly regarding the Eucharist (which Zwingli interpreted as purely symbolic rather than sacramental). By 1519, Zwingli had begun to gather a following in Zurich, and his movement would lead to the Swiss Reformation and eventually to religious conflict in Switzerland.
key_figures: Ulrich Zwingli (Swiss priest and reformer)
Martin Luther (German reformer, independent but contemporary)
Zurich city council (supporters of Zwingli's reforms)

sources.name: Contemporary accounts of the Swiss Reformation

events.certainty_note: Zwingli's beginning of his reformist preaching in 1519 is well-documented, though the exact date is somewhat fluid. Modern scholarship generally accepts 1519 as the beginning of his public reformist activity.

events.title: Montezuma II Receives Cortés in Tenochtitlan; Aztec Empire at Its Height
events.description: In November 1519, Aztec Emperor Montezuma II received Hernán Cortés and his Spanish expedition in the capital city of Tenochtitlan. Montezuma's decision to receive Cortés peacefully, rather than attack him, remains one of the most debated moments in history. Some scholars suggest that Montezuma believed Cortés might be the god Quetzalcoatl returning, as prophesied in Aztec mythology. Others argue that Montezuma was attempting to assess the Spanish threat or to incorporate them into the Aztec tributary system. Whatever his motivations, Montezuma's reception of Cortés allowed the Spanish to enter the city and establish themselves, setting the stage for the conquest that would unfold over the next two years. At this moment, the Aztec Empire appeared to be at the height of its power, with Tenochtitlan being one of the largest cities in the world.
key_figures: Montezuma II (Aztec emperor)
Hernán Cortés (Spanish conquistador)
Malinche (indigenous woman, translator and advisor to Cortés)

sources.name: Bernal Díaz del Castillo, The True History of the Conquest of New Spain
Cortés, Cartas de relación (Letters of Relation)

events.certainty_note: Montezuma's reception of Cortés in November 1519 is well-documented in Spanish and indigenous sources. The date is universally accepted.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative that Montezuma II believed Cortés to be the god Quetzalcoatl (1519-007) is disputed by modern scholarship. While this explanation appears in Spanish sources (particularly Cortés's own letters), it is not supported by indigenous sources and may have been a post-hoc rationalization invented by Spanish chroniclers. Modern scholars suggest that Montezuma's reception of Cortés was more likely motivated by political calculation, curiosity, or an attempt to assess the Spanish threat. Additionally, the characterization of the Aztec Empire as 'at its height' in 1519 is somewhat misleading, as the empire was already facing internal tensions and resistance from tributary states—tensions that Cortés would exploit. The narrative of Spanish military superiority is also complicated by the fact that Cortés's success depended heavily on indigenous allies and on disease rather than Spanish military technology.
historiographic_note: The year 1519 is exceptionally well-documented in European sources, particularly regarding the election of Charles V and the departure of Magellan's expedition. Spanish conquest narratives are abundant but reflect Spanish perspectives; indigenous accounts (particularly the Florentine Codex) provide crucial counterbalance but were compiled decades later. The documentation is heavily weighted toward Europe and the Atlantic world; Asian and African events are less thoroughly recorded in surviving sources. The year marks a pivotal moment in global history—the beginning of European global dominance and the initiation of the conquest of the Americas—but these events were not perceived as interconnected by contemporaries. Leonardo da Vinci's death, while culturally significant, was not perceived as a major historical event at the time.

graph_edges.note: Cortés's landing in February 1519 led to his reception by Montezuma in November 1519.
Charles V's election as Holy Roman Emperor in 1519 occurred in the same year as Magellan's departure.
Magellan's departure in 1519 led to his arrival in the Philippines in 1521.
Zwingli's reformation in Zurich (1519) was contemporary with and independent of Luther's reformation (begun 1517).
